title: Should schools teach the use of obsolete tools?
summary: Argue whether students should learn to use tools and skills the world has stopped needing.
topic: Whether knowledge of obsolete tools should be taught
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether schools should teach how to use obsolete tools (typewriters, slide rules, hand looms, paper maps). Take a position. Use a cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one international example.
hintStarters: In a small school north of Reykjavik…
What an obsolete tool teaches the hand is…
It may well be that the most useful skills are…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thuê ngoài / giao cho thứ khác làm
một bàn tay biết tư duy

sampleText:
In a small school north of Reykjavik, ten-year-olds spend one hour a week on a row of mechanical typewriters older than their teachers. They are not learning to type for a living; the modern keyboards are in the next room. They are learning what it costs to put a sentence on paper when you cannot delete it. By the end of term, most write more slowly than they did before, and choose their words more carefully. The teacher considers this a success.

What an obsolete tool teaches the hand is not nostalgia but constraint. A typewriter punishes the half-thought sentence. A slide rule, used in some Swiss engineering schools well into the 1990s, forces the user to estimate the magnitude of an answer before computing it — a habit that calculators quietly removed from a generation. A paper map, unfolded on a kitchen table, reveals what a phone screen cannot: that this town is reachable on foot and that one isn't. The tool teaches by refusing to do the work for you.

Granted, classroom time is finite, and there is a serious case against using any of it on tools that nobody will earn a living with. Children should leave school able to use the technologies of their century, not its grandparents'. The argument is not that students should master a hand loom instead of a spreadsheet. The argument is that an afternoon a month on something obsolete may quietly teach something the spreadsheet cannot: how patience feels, how mistakes cost, how a finished thing is different from a draft.

What's more, the modern tool tends to hide its own structure. A child who has only ever used a search engine has no way to picture what a librarian was doing for them; a child who has only ever used a GPS has no internal model of distance. The obsolete tool is slower precisely because it exposes the steps that the new tool has folded away. That exposure is the lesson, not the tool.

Even so, the case is not for replacing modern instruction but for protecting a small corner of it. A school day that spends three minutes per topic on screens is a school day that may produce fluent users with no muscle memory. It may well be that the most useful skill a school can pass on is not how to use a tool, but how to know when a tool is doing your thinking for you. An hour on a typewriter, once a week, is a strange but defensible way to teach that.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine a lesson
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What an obsolete tool teaches the hand is not nostalgia but constraint' dùng what-cleft với cấu trúc 'not X but Y' để đảo ngược kỳ vọng của người đọc.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What X teaches is not Y but Z' uses a what-cleft plus 'not … but …' to reverse the reader's likely first guess.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Hedged conclusion with 'It may well be that'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It may well be that the most useful skill a school can pass on is…' đưa ra kết luận chính nhưng vẫn để chỗ cho khả năng sai — đặc trưng của argumentative writing trưởng thành.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It may well be that + clause' offers a main conclusion while still leaving room to be wrong — characteristic of mature argumentative writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong một trường nhỏ ở phía bắc Reykjavik, các em mười tuổi dành một giờ mỗi tuần trên một dãy máy đánh chữ cơ học cũ hơn cả giáo viên của chúng. Các em không học gõ để kiếm sống; bàn phím hiện đại nằm ở phòng bên cạnh. Các em đang học cái giá phải trả để đặt một câu lên giấy khi bạn không thể xóa nó. Đến cuối học kỳ, hầu hết viết chậm hơn so với trước, và chọn từ cẩn thận hơn. Giáo viên coi đây là một thành công.

Điều mà một công cụ lỗi thời dạy cho bàn tay không phải là hoài niệm mà là sự ràng buộc. Máy đánh chữ trừng phạt câu chưa nghĩ kỹ. Một thước tính, được dùng tại một số trường kỹ thuật ở Thụy Sĩ đến tận những năm 1990, buộc người dùng phải ước lượng độ lớn của đáp án trước khi tính toán — một thói quen mà máy tính đã lặng lẽ loại bỏ khỏi một thế hệ. Một bản đồ giấy, trải ra trên bàn bếp, tiết lộ điều mà màn hình điện thoại không thể: rằng thị trấn này có thể đi bộ tới được và thị trấn kia thì không. Công cụ dạy bằng cách từ chối làm việc thay bạn.

Công nhận, thời gian trên lớp là có hạn, và có một lập luận nghiêm túc chống lại việc dùng nó cho các công cụ mà không ai sẽ kiếm sống được. Trẻ em nên ra trường biết dùng công nghệ của thế kỷ mình, không phải của ông bà chúng. Lập luận không phải là học sinh nên thành thạo khung dệt tay thay vì bảng tính. Lập luận là một buổi chiều mỗi tháng dành cho một thứ lỗi thời có thể lặng lẽ dạy điều mà bảng tính không thể: sự kiên nhẫn cảm giác ra sao, lỗi lầm tốn kém như thế nào, một thứ hoàn thiện khác bản nháp ở đâu.

Hơn nữa, công cụ hiện đại có xu hướng giấu đi cấu trúc của chính nó. Một đứa trẻ chỉ biết dùng công cụ tìm kiếm không có cách nào hình dung được người thủ thư đang làm gì cho nó; một đứa trẻ chỉ biết dùng GPS không có mô hình nội tại về khoảng cách. Công cụ lỗi thời chậm hơn chính vì nó bộc lộ các bước mà công cụ mới đã gập lại. Sự bộc lộ đó mới là bài học, không phải công cụ.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận không phải là thay thế giảng dạy hiện đại mà là bảo vệ một góc nhỏ của nó. Một ngày học dành ba phút mỗi chủ đề trên màn hình là một ngày học có thể tạo ra người dùng thành thạo nhưng không có trí nhớ cơ bắp. Rất có thể là kỹ năng hữu ích nhất mà một trường có thể truyền lại không phải là cách dùng một công cụ, mà là cách biết khi nào một công cụ đang suy nghĩ thay bạn. Một giờ trên máy đánh chữ, mỗi tuần một lần, là một cách kỳ lạ nhưng có thể bảo vệ được để dạy điều đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which what-cleft delays the central concept to the end of the sentence?
options: What an obsolete tool teaches the hand is not nostalgia but constraint.
An obsolete tool what teaches not nostalgia hand is constraint.
It is what an obsolete tool that teaches the hand nostalgia constraint.
Teaching an obsolete tool what the hand is constraint nostalgia.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X teaches is not Y but Z' is the standard cleft for contrast.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession opens with the cleanest B2 marker?
options: Granted, classroom time is finite, and there is a serious case against using any of it on tools that nobody will earn a living with.
Of course granted finite the time is classroom serious case against.
Conceding granted time classroom finite no one a tools living earn.
Even granted finite time classroom case against living earn.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, + clause' is the cleanest concession opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive most naturally?
options: A habit that calculators quietly removed from a generation.
A habit which by calculators quietly was removed a generation.
Calculators a habit quietly which removed by a generation.
Quietly calculators removed have which a habit generation.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A uses active voice but is the cleanest version — sometimes active beats clumsy passive.
mcQuestions.hint: Cleanest is best, even if it's active.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces a related additional argument naturally?
options: Whatever moreover, modern tools hide structure.
What's more, the modern tool tends to hide its own structure.
In conclusion the modern tool hides structure.
Throughout history all modern tools hid structure.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What's more, …' is the cleanest additive transition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'tends to' as a hedge correctly?
options: The modern tool tends to hide its own structure.
Tends to the modern tool hide structure own.
The modern tool is tend hide structure own its.
Tend to hide modern tool own the structure its.
mcQuestions.explanation: Subject + tends to + base verb is the correct hedging form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'precisely' to sharpen the cause?
options: The obsolete tool is slower precisely because it exposes the steps that the new tool has folded away.
Precisely the obsolete tool because steps slower exposes new tool folded.
It is precisely slower obsolete tool the steps folded which.
Slower precisely the obsolete tool steps new folded away which.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Precisely because + clause' tightens a causal claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best continues a sentence ending '…that nobody will earn a living with'?
options: In today's world, learning to type is important.
Children should leave school able to use the technologies of their century, not its grandparents'.
It is important to note that nobody earns a living typing.
Throughout history, students have always learned tools.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B continues the concession with a concrete contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option that develops the argument with specific contrast.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'it may well be that' correctly?
options: It may well be that the most useful skill a school can pass on is not how to use a tool, but how to know when a tool is doing your thinking for you.
May well it is the most useful school skill pass on.
Most useful skill it may well be a school passes on.
Be that may well it the school useful skill pass.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It may well be that + clause' is the correct hedged-conclusion form.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It may well be that + subject + verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'even so' to pivot back to the main argument?
options: Even so, the case is not for replacing modern instruction but for protecting a small corner of it.
Even so the modern is replacement instruction case for protect.
Modern even so replacement instruction not corner protect small.
Instruction modern not protect corner the case even so.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, + clause' acknowledges a concession and pivots back.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even so, + clause' belongs at the start of the pivoting sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which subtle register choice fits a B2 essay best?
options: Typewriters are super cool lol
By the end of term, most write more slowly than they did before, and choose their words more carefully.
Old typewriter = better than computer obvi
Type slow = think slow LOL
mcQuestions.explanation: Only B keeps formal, considered register with specific claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses participle-like reduced clause correctly?
options: A paper map, unfolded on a kitchen table, reveals what a phone screen cannot.
Unfolded a paper map kitchen table reveals which phone screen.
A paper map having unfolded kitchen table phone screen reveals.
On the kitchen table unfolded a paper map which reveals phone screen cannot.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A paper map, unfolded on a kitchen table, …' uses a reduced participle clause as a parenthetical.
mcQuestions.hint: The participle clause sits between commas.

options: muscle memory
muscle constraint
muscle tactile
muscle fluency
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Muscle memory' is a fixed collocation meaning embodied skill.
mcQuestions.hint: Which two words go together as a phrase?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence ends an essay with a concrete, defensible final image?
options: In conclusion, obsolete tools should be taught because they are good.
An hour on a typewriter, once a week, is a strange but defensible way to teach that.
Throughout history, humans have used many tools.
It is important to note that tools matter for learning.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B ends with a specific, deliberate image that closes the argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the ending that is specific and self-aware.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure correctly?
options: How patience feels, how mistakes cost, how a finished thing is different from a draft.
Patience how feeling, mistake how costed, finished how draft different a thing from is.
Feeling how patience, costing how mistake, being how finished thing a draft.
Patience feel how, cost mistake how, finished thing how a draft different.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A repeats 'how + clause' three times — clean parallelism.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for three 'how + subject + verb' clauses.

options: Throughout history, schools have always taught tools.
In today's fast-paced world, students need many skills.
It is important to note that learning is important.
In a small school north of Reykjavik, ten-year-olds spend one hour a week on a row of mechanical typewriters older than their teachers.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option D opens with a specific place, age, and image — the others are platitudes.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If your school could keep one obsolete tool in regular use for the next ten years, which would you choose, and what would you hope it teaches that no modern tool does?
